narrative_blocks.header: He was godson of a president
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Taft Snowdon was the grandnephew and godson of President William Howard Taft. He and his mother moved into the White House for 18 months during the Taft presidency. When Taft later became Chief Justice, Snowdon often visited and sat with the justices at the deliberating roundtable.

narrative_blocks.header: He played professional football AND professional baseball
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale, Charles Taft Snowdon played fullback for the Pittsburgh Steelers and first base for the Pittsburgh Pirates. Playing professionally in two major sports is extraordinarily rare. He later broadcast radio for a Pittsburgh affiliate and for West Virginia University's championship basketball teams.

narrative_blocks.header: He saw Mussolini hanging upside down
narrative_blocks.body: As a U.S. Army second lieutenant in WWII, Charles Taft Snowdon was present when Mussolini was executed and his body displayed in Milan in April 1945. 'That was one of his favorite stories,' his wife Margaret told the Orlando Sentinel in 1992. 'He led a charmed life. He loved to tell stories, and he had a great sense of humor.'

narrative_blocks.header: He was godson of a president
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Taft Snowdon was the grandnephew and godson of President William Howard Taft. He and his mother moved into the White House for 18 months during the Taft presidency. When Taft later became Chief Justice, Snowdon often visited and sat with the justices at the deliberating roundtable.

narrative_blocks.header: He played professional football AND professional baseball
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale, Charles Taft Snowdon played fullback for the Pittsburgh Steelers and first base for the Pittsburgh Pirates. Playing professionally in two major sports is extraordinarily rare. He later broadcast radio for a Pittsburgh affiliate and for West Virginia University's championship basketball teams.

narrative_blocks.header: He saw Mussolini hanging upside down
narrative_blocks.body: As a U.S. Army second lieutenant in WWII, Charles Taft Snowdon was present when Mussolini was executed and his body displayed in Milan in April 1945. 'That was one of his favorite stories,' his wife Margaret told the Orlando Sentinel in 1992. 'He led a charmed life. He loved to tell stories, and he had a great sense of humor.'

Louise Witherbee Taft Snowdon died January 23, 1926 in Augusta, Georgia at age 37. Her son Charles was fourteen. He grew up to play professional football for the Pittsburgh Steelers and professional baseball for the Pittsburgh Pirates, to live in the White House as the godson of his great-uncle President Taft, and to see Mussolini's body hanging upside down in Milan in 1945.

notable.notable_blurb: Name partner at Cadwalader Wickersham & Taft

cross_connections.display_label: His father co-founded Skull and Bones with this man — Henry joined the same society a generation later.
Both were Bohemian Club members — two Hooker descendants in the same San Francisco social institution.
Later-generation American antitrust leader; Nixon FTC Chairman 1970-1973

narrative_blocks.header: He resigned from the Japan Society the day
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Waters Taft was President of the Japan Society of New York for two separate periods spanning nearly two decades. He resigned in December 1941 — in the wake of the attack on Pearl Harbor. He had spent years advocating for US-Japan diplomatic relations and writing extensively on Japan; his sudden resignation marked the end of that advocacy.

narrative_blocks.header: At Bohemian Grove, he described what the Club
narrative_blocks.body: At an American Bar Association convention trip to Bohemian Grove, Henry Waters Taft said: 'The Bohemian Club unites the world of big business and the world of fine art, literature and cultural ideals — and could only exist in a community such as this.' His brother William Howard Taft was also fond of the Club.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
